inlineContent.text: A flexible keyboard layout is an important part of a software keyboard, and must consider many factors like the current locale, device, screen orientation, user preferences, etc.
In KeyboardKit, a
describes the full keyboard layout, which can use an
to define the input keys, then surround them with additional keys.
KeyboardKit has a
protocol that is implemented by classes that can create keyboard layouts, as well as many base classes that create standard layouts that can then be customized.
unlocks localized input sets and layout services for all locales, as well as additional sets like
and
, and support for iPad Pro. Information about Pro features can be found at the end of this article.
KeyboardKit has a
type that is also a namespace for other layout-related types like
, etc.
An
specifies the input keys of a keyboard. It makes it easy to define different input keys for the same keyboard layout.
KeyboardKit comes with some pre-defined input sets, like
and
. KeyboardKit Pro unlocks more input sets, e.g. QWERTZ, AZERTY, and specific input sets for each locale.
A
specifies the full set of keys on a keyboard. Layouts vary greatly for different device types, screen orientations, locales, keyboard configurations, etc.
For instance,
most

iOS keyboards have 3 input rows, with input keys that are surrounded by action keys, as well as a bottom row with a space bar and contextual action keys. This is however not true for all locales, where the layout can vary greatly.
Given all this, the layout engine has to be flexible. KeyboardKit has a
that generates layouts at runtime, based on many different factors. Layout services can use any information to tweak any part of a layout at any time.
KeyboardKit automatically creates an instance of
and injects it into
. You can replace it at any time, as described further down, or inject custom services into it.
You can easily resolve various
implementations with these shorthands, sorted by relevance:
(👑 KeyboardKit Pro)

content.text: How to create a custom layout service

inlineContent.text: You can create a custom layout service to customize the layout for certain locales or devices, or define a completely custom layout.
You can implement the
protocol from scratch, or inherit and customize any of the
, or
base classes, for instance:

code: // Never use array indices if it can cause a crash.
override func keyboardLayout(for context: KeyboardContext) -> KeyboardLayout {

inlineContent.text: The
and
base classes have different functions that you can override, and the
model also provodes ways to modify the layout, although they are a bit complicated at the moment.
To use your custom service instead of the standard one, just inject it into
by replacing its
property:
This will make KeyboardKit use your custom implementation instead of the standard one.

for every locale in your license, which are then injected into the main

and makes it easier to handle layouts.

that can be used to generate iPad Pro-specific layouts. It’s used by most locales, and will be added to more over time.
